name: VS Code
description: Remote VS Code development environment via tunnel
setup: $SUDO apt-get update && $SUDO apt-get install -y gnupg software-properties-common apt-transport-https wget && wget -qO- https://packages.microsoft.com/keys/microsoft.asc | gpg --dearmor > packages.microsoft.gpg && $SUDO install -o root -g root -m 644 packages.microsoft.gpg /usr/share/keyrings/ && echo "deb [arch=amd64 signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/packages.microsoft.gpg] https://packages.microsoft.com/repos/code stable main" | $SUDO tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/vscode.list && $SUDO apt-get update && $SUDO apt-get install -y code
_comment_ports: VS Code tunnel does not expose fixed local ports; the tunnel URL is generated dynamically by the VS Code CLI.
modal_subtitle: Follow the steps below to authenticate and open your VS Code session.

instructions.title: Step 1: Authorize VS Code
instructions.description: When the VS Code service starts, it may print an authorization code. Copy the code below (when available), go to https://github.com/login/device and complete the sign-in flow in your browser.
instructions.placeholder: Waiting for auth code from provider logs...

instructions.title: Step 2: Open VS Code
instructions.description: After you finish authorization, the URL will appear here. Use it to open the remote environment in your browser-based VS Code.
instructions.placeholder: Waiting for VS Code to print a URL in the provider logs...

name: Jupyter Notebook
description: Jupyter Lab notebook environment via ngrok tunnel
command: ngrok config add-authtoken $NGROK_AUTH_TOKEN; jupyter lab --ip=0.0.0.0 --port=8888 --no-browser --allow-root --NotebookApp.token='' --NotebookApp.password='' --notebook-dir=~ > /tmp/jupyter.log 2>&1 & sleep 3 && ngrok http 8888 --log=stdout 2>&1 | tee /tmp/ngrok.log; tail -f /tmp/jupyter.log /tmp/ngrok.log

env_parameters.help_text: Your ngrok authentication token. You can get your token from https://dashboard.ngrok.com/get-started/your-authtoken.

logic.tunnel: command -v ngrok >/dev/null 2>&1 || (curl -sSL https://ngrok-agent.s3.amazonaws.com/ngrok.asc | $SUDO tee /etc/apt/trusted.gpg.d/ngrok.asc >/dev/null && echo "deb https://ngrok-agent.s3.amazonaws.com bookworm main" | $SUDO tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/ngrok.list && $SUDO apt-get update && $SUDO apt-get install -y ngrok); ngrok config add-authtoken $NGROK_AUTH_TOKEN; ngrok http 8888 --log=stdout 2>&1 | tee /tmp/ngrok.log

_comment_ports: Jupyter Lab listens on port 8888. The ngrok tunnel exposes this port externally.
modal_subtitle: Access your Jupyter notebook through the URL below.

instructions.title: Access Jupyter Notebook
instructions.description: Once ready, click the link below to open your Jupyter notebook in your browser.
instructions.placeholder: Waiting for service to start. The URL will appear here once the connection is available...

name: vLLM Server
description: vLLM OpenAI-compatible API server via ngrok tunnel (plus Open WebUI)
setup: if ! command -v uv >/dev/null 2>&1; then curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh; fi && export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$HOME/.cargo/bin:$PATH" && uv venv ~/vllm-venv --seed --python 3.11 && . ~/vllm-venv/bin/activate && uv pip install "vllm>=0.13.0" "transformers>=4.57.3" qwen-vl-utils==0.0.14 open-webui
command:
ngrok config add-authtoken $NGROK_AUTH_TOKEN; ~/vllm-venv/bin/python -u -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server --model $MODEL_NAME --tensor-parallel-size $TP_SIZE --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 --gpu-memory-utilization 0.9 > /tmp/vllm.log 2>&1 & sleep 10 && OPENAI_API_BASE_URL=http://127.0.0.1:8000/v1 OPENAI_API_KEY=dummy WEBUI_AUTH=false ~/vllm-venv/bin/open-webui serve --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8080 > /tmp/openwebui.log 2>&1 & sleep 5 && printf '%s
' 'version: 2' 'authtoken: '"$NGROK_AUTH_TOKEN" 'tunnels:' '  vllm:' '    proto: http' '    addr: 8000' '  openwebui:' '    proto: http' '    addr: 8080' > ~/ngrok-vllm.yml && ngrok start --all --config ~/ngrok-vllm.yml --log=stdout 2>&1 | tee /tmp/ngrok.log; tail -f /tmp/vllm.log /tmp/openwebui.log /tmp/ngrok.log

env_parameters.help_text: Number of GPUs for tensor parallelism (default: 1)
Your ngrok authentication token. You can get your token from https://dashboard.ngrok.com/get-started/your-authtoken.

logic.core: ~/vllm-venv/bin/python -u -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server --model $MODEL_NAME --tensor-parallel-size $TP_SIZE --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 --gpu-memory-utilization 0.9 > /tmp/vllm.log 2>&1 & sleep 10 && OPENAI_API_BASE_URL=http://127.0.0.1:8000/v1 OPENAI_API_KEY=dummy WEBUI_AUTH=false ~/vllm-venv/bin/open-webui serve --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8080 > /tmp/openwebui.log 2>&1 & sleep 5
logic.tunnel:
command -v ngrok >/dev/null 2>&1 || (curl -sSL https://ngrok-agent.s3.amazonaws.com/ngrok.asc | $SUDO tee /etc/apt/trusted.gpg.d/ngrok.asc >/dev/null && echo "deb https://ngrok-agent.s3.amazonaws.com bookworm main" | $SUDO tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/ngrok.list && $SUDO apt-get update && $SUDO apt-get install -y ngrok); ngrok config add-authtoken $NGROK_AUTH_TOKEN; printf '%s
' 'version: 2' 'authtoken: '"$NGROK_AUTH_TOKEN" 'tunnels:' '  vllm:' '    proto: http' '    addr: 8000' '  openwebui:' '    proto: http' '    addr: 8080' > ~/ngrok-vllm.yml && ngrok start --all --config ~/ngrok-vllm.yml --log=stdout 2>&1 | tee /tmp/ngrok.log

_comment_ports: vLLM API on 8000, Open WebUI on 8080. Both tunneled via ngrok.
modal_subtitle: Access your vLLM API server through the URL below.

instructions.title: Access vLLM API Server
instructions.description: Once ready, use the URL below to access your vLLM server. The vLLM server provides an OpenAI-compatible API endpoint.
instructions.placeholder: Waiting for service to start. The URL will appear here once the connection is available...

instructions.title: Access Open WebUI
instructions.description: Use the URL below to open Open WebUI connected to this vLLM server. This provides a browser-based chat UI backed by the same vLLM API.
instructions.placeholder: Waiting for the Open WebUI service to start...

name: Ollama Server
description: Ollama model server via ngrok tunnel (plus Open WebUI)
setup: if ! command -v uv >/dev/null 2>&1; then curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh; fi && export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$HOME/.cargo/bin:$PATH" && uv venv ~/ollama-venv --seed --python 3.11 && . ~/ollama-venv/bin/activate && uv pip install open-webui && if ! command -v ollama >/dev/null 2>&1; then curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh; fi && if [ "$(uname)" != "Darwin" ]; then $SUDO apt-get update && $SUDO apt-get install -y pciutils lshw; fi
command:
ngrok config add-authtoken $NGROK_AUTH_TOKEN; export OLLAMA_HOST=0.0.0.0:11434 && ollama serve > /tmp/ollama.log 2>&1 & sleep 3 && ollama pull $MODEL_NAME > /tmp/ollama-pull.log 2>&1 & sleep 5 && OLLAMA_BASE_URL=http://127.0.0.1:11434 WEBUI_AUTH=false ~/ollama-venv/bin/open-webui serve --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8080 > /tmp/openwebui.log 2>&1 & sleep 5 && printf '%s
' 'version: 2' 'authtoken: '"$NGROK_AUTH_TOKEN" 'tunnels:' '  ollama:' '    proto: http' '    addr: 11434' '  openwebui:' '    proto: http' '    addr: 8080' > ~/ngrok-ollama.yml && ngrok start --all --config ~/ngrok-ollama.yml --log=stdout 2>&1 | tee /tmp/ngrok.log; tail -f /tmp/ollama.log /tmp/ollama-pull.log /tmp/openwebui.log /tmp/ngrok.log

env_parameters.help_text: Ollama model name (e.g. llama2, mistral, codellama). Use "ollama pull <model>" to download models.
Your ngrok authentication token. You can get your token from https://dashboard.ngrok.com/get-started/your-authtoken.

logic.core: export OLLAMA_HOST=0.0.0.0:11434 && ollama serve > /tmp/ollama.log 2>&1 & sleep 3 && ollama pull $MODEL_NAME > /tmp/ollama-pull.log 2>&1 & sleep 5 && OLLAMA_BASE_URL=http://127.0.0.1:11434 WEBUI_AUTH=false ~/ollama-venv/bin/open-webui serve --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8080 > /tmp/openwebui.log 2>&1 & sleep 5
logic.tunnel:
command -v ngrok >/dev/null 2>&1 || (curl -sSL https://ngrok-agent.s3.amazonaws.com/ngrok.asc | $SUDO tee /etc/apt/trusted.gpg.d/ngrok.asc >/dev/null && echo "deb https://ngrok-agent.s3.amazonaws.com bookworm main" | $SUDO tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/ngrok.list && $SUDO apt-get update && $SUDO apt-get install -y ngrok); ngrok config add-authtoken $NGROK_AUTH_TOKEN; printf '%s
' 'version: 2' 'authtoken: '"$NGROK_AUTH_TOKEN" 'tunnels:' '  ollama:' '    proto: http' '    addr: 11434' '  openwebui:' '    proto: http' '    addr: 8080' > ~/ngrok-ollama.yml && ngrok start --all --config ~/ngrok-ollama.yml --log=stdout 2>&1 | tee /tmp/ngrok.log

_comment_ports: Ollama API on 11434, Open WebUI on 8080. Both tunneled via ngrok.
modal_subtitle: Access your Ollama API server through the URL below.

instructions.title: Access Ollama API Server
instructions.description: Once ready, use the URL below to access your Ollama server. The Ollama server provides an OpenAI-compatible API endpoint.
instructions.placeholder: Waiting for service to start. The URL will appear here once the connection is available...

instructions.title: Access Open WebUI
instructions.description: Use the URL below to open Open WebUI connected to this Ollama server. This gives you a convenient browser chat UI backed by the same Ollama API.
instructions.placeholder: Waiting for the Open WebUI service to start...

name: SSH
description: SSH access via ngrok TCP tunnel
command: ngrok config add-authtoken $NGROK_AUTH_TOKEN; echo USER_ID=$(whoami 2>/dev/null || basename $HOME 2>/dev/null || echo ''); ngrok tcp 22 --log=stdout 2>&1 | tee /tmp/ngrok.log; tail -f /tmp/ngrok.log

env_parameters.help_text: Your ngrok authentication token. Note: You may need to add a payment method to your ngrok account (it won't be charged, but it's necessary for SSH connections). You can get your token from https://dashboard.ngrok.com/get-started/your-authtoken.

logic.tunnel: command -v ngrok >/dev/null 2>&1 || (curl -sSL https://ngrok-agent.s3.amazonaws.com/ngrok.asc | $SUDO tee /etc/apt/trusted.gpg.d/ngrok.asc >/dev/null && echo "deb https://ngrok-agent.s3.amazonaws.com bookworm main" | $SUDO tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/ngrok.list && $SUDO apt-get update && $SUDO apt-get install -y ngrok); ngrok config add-authtoken $NGROK_AUTH_TOKEN; ngrok tcp 22 --log=stdout 2>&1 | tee /tmp/ngrok.log

_comment_ports: SSH runs on port 22, tunneled via ngrok TCP tunnel.
modal_subtitle: Access your remote machine via SSH through the ngrok tunnel.

instructions.title: SSH Command
instructions.description: Use the command below to connect to your remote machine.
instructions.placeholder: Waiting for tunnel to start. The SSH command will appear here once ngrok creates the tunnel...

instructions.title: Usage Instructions
instructions.template:
1. Download your organization's SSH key from Team Settings
2. Save the key file (e.g., ~/org_ssh_key_{{team_id}})
3. Set permissions: chmod 600 ~/org_ssh_key_{{team_id}}
4. Copy the SSH command above
5. Open your terminal
6. Paste and run the command

name: Ollama + Gradio

env_parameters.help_text: Ollama model name (e.g. llama2, mistral, codellama).
